summary: Joe Reis argues that while AI dramatically reduces the cost of producing content and code, the cost of creating high-quality, deep work remains as high as ever. He challenges the notion that citizen developers and vibe coding will replace professional engineers, predicting instead a 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware' as prototypes inevitably become production systems. The key to thriving is pairing AI acceleration with genuine craftsmanship and mastery, not abandoning expertise altogether.
key_points: AI reduces content creation costs to near zero, but quality and depth remain expensive
Citizen developer and no-code movements failed because people struggle to understand and communicate problems, not because of tooling limitations
Prototypes have a habit of becoming production systems, creating technical debt
Engineers are problem solvers, not just code writers - AI frees them for higher-value architectural and business-facing work
The future belongs to those who combine AI tools with genuine mastery
money_quotes: The cost of creating content (writing, code, etc.) is zero. The cost to produce high-quality content with depth is still as high as ever.
In what appears to be one of the greatest bouts of cognitive dissonance I've ever seen, this excitement also reveals an existential dread that AI will eliminate their jobs.
A big reason these trends never achieved critical mass is pretty simple. People generally suck at understanding and communicating the problem they're trying to solve.
I predict a Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware will ensue. More apps, more problems.
AI makes respecting and investing in your craft more important than ever. The future belongs to those who pair AI with mastery, not to those who abandon mastery altogether.
key_insight: AI commoditizes the production of mediocre work while making genuine craftsmanship and deep expertise more valuable than ever.
